rationale.R1.1: The contribution claim—a novel curvature-adaptive solver that outperforms PCG—is the load-bearing thesis; assessing whether that framing is coherent and defensible given what is actually described is required to determine if the work 'holds up' at all.
rationale.R1.2: 'Solid' applied to a mathematical artifact most directly means logical and algebraic consistency. The described step alpha_k = (r_k^T r_k)/(r_k^T A r_k) is the classical steepest-descent formula, not a novel curvature-adaptive schedule, creating a direct contradiction between the key-insight claim and the method as written. The stated O(kappa·log(1/eps)) bound matches PCG's rate only if PCG's rate is misquoted (PCG achieves O(sqrt(kappa)·log(1/eps))); steepest descent is strictly worse. These internal inconsistencies make R1.2 load-bearing.
rationale.R1.3: The paper asserts empirical superiority over Jacobi-preconditioned PCG on three benchmarks, yet the described method is standard steepest descent, which converges more slowly than PCG on ill-conditioned systems. Verifying that the stated experimental claim is reachable from the described algorithm is load-bearing.
rationale.R1.4: If alpha_k is the classical steepest-descent step, the novelty claim collapses entirely. Determining whether any genuine delta over prior work exists is central to whether the artifact is defensible as a research contribution.
